# mongo_document

This file gives LLMs a fuller mental model of the `mongo_document` package family.

## What This Package Family Is For

The goal is to make MongoDB usage feel natural in Dart code.

Example:

```dart
final post = await Post(body: 'Hello world').save();

final updated = await post.copyWith(
  body: 'Updated body',
).save();

final drafts = await Posts.findMany(
  (q) => q.status.eq('draft'),
  limit: 20,
);
```

The developer-facing surface is Dart-first. The runtime and storage details stay underneath.

## Package Roles

- `mongo_document_annotation`
  - model annotations
  - converters
  - `MongoDbConnection.initialize(...)`
  - lookup types
  - projection types
  - shared normalization helpers
- `mongo_document`
  - generates CRUD helpers, query builders, projection classes, and collection APIs
- `mongo_document_db_driver`
  - low-level database driver package

## Startup Contract

Initialize Mongo once at startup:

```dart
await MongoDbConnection.initialize(uri);
```

Generated helpers default to that shared connection:

```dart
final post = await Posts.findById(postId);
```

If needed, helpers can target another `Db` explicitly:

```dart
final analyticsDb = await Db.create(analyticsUri);
await analyticsDb.open();

final post = await Posts.findById(postId, db: analyticsDb);
```

## Model Contract

Typical model:

```dart
@MongoDocument(collection: 'posts')
@JsonSerializable(fieldRename: FieldRename.snake, explicitToJson: true)
class Post {
  Post({
    @ObjectIdConverter() @JsonKey(name: '_id') this.id,
    this.body,
    this.status,
  });

  final ObjectId? id;
  final String? body;
  final String? status;

  factory Post.fromJson(Map<String, dynamic> json) => _$PostFromJson(json);
  Map<String, dynamic> toJson() => _$PostToJson(this);
}
```

Freezed is common because `copyWith()` is useful, but plain Dart classes are supported too.

## What Gets Generated

For a model like `Post`, generation typically produces:

- `Posts`
- `QPost`
- `PostFields`
- `PostProjections`
- nested projection classes like `PostAuthorProjections`
- instance helpers like `save()`, `saveChanges()`, and `delete()`

## Save Semantics

### `save()`

- inserts when the model has no id
- updates when it already exists
- returns the saved model

### `saveChanges()`

- same immutable-update flow
- more explicit update intent at the call site

### Partial immutable updates

This package supports:

```dart
final updated = await post.copyWith(
  body: 'Updated body',
).save();
```

and

```dart
final updated = await post.copyWith(
  body: 'Updated body',
).saveChanges();
```

When the document originally came from the generated API, the package keeps enough snapshot information to update only changed fields instead of blindly writing the full document back.

If the patch is truly dynamic, use collection helpers like `updateOneFromMap(...)`.

## Typed Object References

Prefer fields like:

```dart
Account? author
```

instead of:

```dart
ObjectId? authorId
```

Why:

- the field reads better in Dart
- queries stay readable: `p.author.id.eq(...)`
- the package still stores a plain `ObjectId` in Mongo
- the same field can hydrate into a richer nested model during projections and lookups

Write path:

- `author` reduces to `author._id`

Read path:

- normal reads rewrap stored ids into the typed Dart field shape
- richer reads can populate the same field into a fuller nested model

See [docs/object-references.md](docs/object-references.md).

## Lookups vs Projections

### Lookups

Use lookups when MongoDB should fetch related data in the same read.

Examples:

- one related object: `Lookup.single(...)`
- related list: `Lookup.array(...)`
- boolean relationship: `Lookup.boolean(...)`
- related count: `Lookup.count(...)`

### Projections

Use projections when you only want some fields back.

Examples:

- card/list screens
- dashboard summaries
- permission checks
- trimming nested models

### Important distinction

- lookup decides whether related data is fetched and what shape it has
- projection decides which fields survive in the final payload

For typed object references, a nested projection alone can often be enough to trigger the default reference materialization for that field.

Add an explicit lookup when you need custom join behavior such as:

- `unsetFields`
- `where`
- nested lookups
- boolean/count/array result shape

Write lookup fields using Dart names like `author` or `user`. The package remaps them to stored Mongo keys using `@JsonKey(...)` and `fieldRename`.

See [docs/lookups-and-projections.md](docs/lookups-and-projections.md).

## Query Builder Contract

Generated query builders should read naturally:

```dart
final drafts = await Posts.findMany(
  (q) => q.status.eq('draft'),
);
```

Typed refs also work naturally:

```dart
final posts = await CrossPosts.findMany(
  (p) => p.author.id.eq(currentUser.id),
);
```

That query still maps to the stored Mongo field correctly behind the scenes.

## Field Mapping Contract

This package respects:

- `@JsonKey(name: ...)`
- `@JsonSerializable(fieldRename: ...)`

So a Dart field can stay:

```dart
Workspace? workspace
```

while Mongo stores:

```json
{ "work_space": ... }
```

Generated CRUD, lookups, and query builders should use the Dart field name and let the package map it.

## Regeneration Rules

Run generation again after changing:

- fields
- `@JsonKey(...)`
- `fieldRename`
- lookups
- projections
- nested refs

Command:

```bash
dart run build_runner build --delete-conflicting-outputs
```

- [Troubleshooting](docs/troubleshooting.md)
- [Recipes](docs/recipes/README.md)
